summary: Charity Majors reflects on her relationship with pain as a career growth strategy, from brute-forcing her way past public speaking terror to her painful tenure as CEO of Honeycomb. She concludes that while leaning into acute pain can build skills, chronic pain in a role you hate will only diminish you—sometimes the only way to succeed is to redefine success.
key_points: Brute-forced through public speaking fear by giving 38 talks in two years until it became an ordinary skill
Stepped down as CEO of Honeycomb after realizing chronic misery was not a problem she could power through
Distinguishes between acute pain (useful teacher) and chronic pain (destructive hijacker of reward pathways)
Warns against treating misery as a virtue—pain narrows your vision and shuts down creativity
The wisdom to know which pain to lean into and which to walk away from is the real career skill
money_quotes: Pain is a terrific teacher, but pain is an acute response. Chronic pain will hijack your reward pathways, your perspective, your relationships, and every other productive system and leave them stunted.
Misery is not a virtue. Pain is awful. That's why it's so powerful and primal.

Leaning in to pain can be powerful if you have the agency and ability to change it, or practice it to mastery, or even just adapt your own emotional responses to it. If you don't or you can't, leaning in to pain will kill you. Having the wisdom to know the difference is everything.
Turns out not every problem can be powered through on a high pain tolerance. The collateral damage starts to rack up. Sometimes the only way to succeed is to redefine success.
Everything is hard when you're in pain. Your creative brain shuts down. Basic life functions become impossible tests.
key_insight: Leaning into acute pain builds skills, but chronic pain in a misaligned role will destroy your capacity—knowing which battles to power through and which to walk away from is the defining career wisdom.
